full_response:
We want  

r_* = ∫ r \, \dfrac{dr}{\,r-2GM\,}.                                   (1)

1.  Evaluate the integrand  

   r = (r-2GM) + 2GM  ⇒  \dfrac{r}{r-2GM} = 1 + \dfrac{2GM}{r-2GM}.   (2)

2.  Integrate term-by-term  

   r_* = ∫ 1\,dr + 2GM ∫ \dfrac{dr}{r-2GM}  
        = r + 2GM \ln|r-2GM| + C.                                     (3)

   One usually absorbs the arbitrary constant C by writing the log in dimensionless
   form:

   r_* = r + 2GM \ln\!\left|\dfrac{r}{2GM}-1\right|.                  (4)

3.  Behaviour near the horizon r → 2GM  

   As r → 2GM^{+} (from outside), |r/2GM-1| → 0^{+} and ln → −∞, so  

   r_* → −∞.                                                          (5)

   The same divergence (to −∞) occurs when the horizon is approached from inside.

4.  Why “tortoise” coordinate?  
   The derivative dr_*/dr = r/(r-2GM) blows up at r = 2GM, so an object that moves a
   finite radial distance dr near the horizon covers an *infinitely* large distance
   in the r_* coordinate.  Consequently motion in r_* slows to a crawl as one nears
   the horizon—like a tortoise—hence the name.

\boxed{\,r_* = r + 2GM \ln\!\left|\dfrac{r}{2GM}-1\right|,\quad r_*\xrightarrow{\,r\to 2GM\,}-\infty\,}

Confidence: 85%
